name: Sohrab
description: Sohrab is a tragic hero in Persian mythology, primarily known from Ferdowsi's epic poem, the Shahnameh (Book of Kings). He is the son of Rostam, the most celebrated hero of Persia, and Tahmineh, a princess from Turan. Sohrab's story is marked by themes of duty, fate, and the devastating consequences of ignorance and war. He grows up unaware of his father's identity, and Rostam is similarly unaware of his son. Sohrab is depicted as a formidable warrior, possessing immense strength and skill, eager to prove himself and establish his own kingdom. His ambition and martial prowess lead him into conflict with the Persian army, culminating in a fateful duel with his own father. The tragic irony of their unknowingly fighting each other forms the core of his narrative, highlighting the futility and sorrow inherent in war and the devastating impact of missed connections and mistaken identities. Sohrab embodies the qualities of a noble warrior, but his story serves as a cautionary tale about the destructive power of ignorance and the profound loss that can result from familial separation and conflict.
subtitle: The Tragic Warrior

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Sohrab
keyMyths.description: Rostam, while on a hunting trip in Turan, meets and falls in love with Princess Tahmineh. After a night together, Rostam leaves, giving Tahmineh an armband to give to their child, should it be a boy. He instructs her to tie it to the child's arm as a sign of his lineage. Tahmineh gives birth to Sohrab, concealing Rostam's identity from him, fearing that Rostam would take him away. This sets the stage for the tragic events that unfold later in Sohrab's life.

keyMyths.title: Sohrab's Challenge
keyMyths.description: Driven by ambition and a desire to prove himself, Sohrab decides to invade Persia. He believes that by overthrowing the current Shah, he can pave the way for his father, Rostam, to rule. He leads an army against the Persian forces, demonstrating his exceptional strength and combat skills, quickly establishing himself as a major threat. This invasion sets the stage for the fateful encounter with his father.

keyMyths.title: The Duel with Rostam
keyMyths.description: The climax of Sohrab's story is his duel with Rostam. Unbeknownst to either of them, they are father and son. Rostam, hesitant to reveal his identity, fights Sohrab in a grueling battle. Rostam eventually defeats Sohrab, mortally wounding him. As Sohrab lies dying, he reveals the armband given to him by his mother, revealing his true identity as Rostam's son. Rostam is overcome with grief and remorse, realizing the tragic mistake he has made. This scene is considered one of the most heart-wrenching in Persian literature.

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Sohrab and Rostam, providing a detailed account of their lives and tragic encounter.
sources.text: The primary source for the story of Sohrab and Rostam, providing a detailed account of their lives and tragic encounter.

sources.author: Dick Davis

sources.text: A modern English translation of Ferdowsi's Shahnameh, offering accessibility to a wider audience.

sources.text: An academic analysis of the Shahnameh, exploring the themes and characters within the epic, including Sohrab and Rostam.

associations.name: Youthful Ardor
associations.significance: Represents the passionate intensity and impetuousness of youth, leading to both heroic feats and tragic mistakes.

associations.name: Sword
associations.significance: Symbolizes Sohrab's martial prowess and his role as a warrior, but also the instrument of his own demise.

associations.name: Father-Son Conflict
associations.significance: Represents the tragic consequences of unrecognized kinship and the destructive power of familial misunderstanding.

associations.name: Battlefield
associations.significance: Highlights the setting where Sohrab's fate unfolds, emphasizing the brutality and inevitability of war.

associations.name: Inevitable Death
associations.significance: Illustrates the power of destiny and the tragic limitations of human agency in the face of predetermined outcomes.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: Sohrab's unwavering loyalty to his mother and his kingdom, even at his own expense, showcases a noble but ultimately fatal trait.

associations.name: Unrecognition
associations.significance: The theme of not being recognized by his father Rostam is a central tragedy, symbolizing missed connections and the pain of ignorance.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Sohrab, Telemachus undertakes a journey in search of his father, facing challenges and demonstrating courage in his own right, although with a different outcome.
Connla, son of Cú Chulainn, shares a similar tragic fate, meeting his father in battle and being slain unknowingly, highlighting the theme of unrecognized kinship.
Baldr's story, though different in specifics, echoes the theme of a hero destined for a premature and tragic death, influenced by fate and misunderstanding.
Abhimanyu from the Mahabharata, like Sohrab, is a young warrior of immense skill who faces a powerful enemy and meets a tragic end, showcasing courage and martial prowess.

worshipPractices.name: Epic Recitations
worshipPractices.description: While not a deity, Sohrab's story is kept alive through the recitation of the Shahnameh in various cultural and social gatherings, particularly in Iran and among Persian-speaking communities. These recitations serve as a form of veneration, honoring his memory and the moral lessons embedded in his tale.

cultural.modernLegacy: Sohrab's tragic tale continues to resonate in modern Persian culture, inspiring works of literature, art, and music. His story is often used to explore themes of identity, fate, and the consequences of war, serving as a cautionary tale about the importance of communication and understanding. The character remains a symbol of youthful heroism and the poignant beauty of tragic destiny.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Upbringing
extendedContent.content: Sohrab's early life is shrouded in secrecy. Born to Rostam and Tahmineh in Turan, he grows up without knowing his father's true identity. Tahmineh fears that Rostam's enemies would target her son if his parentage were known, so she raises him in isolation, instilling in him a sense of duty and martial prowess. Sohrab quickly develops into a formidable warrior, surpassing his peers in strength and skill. Driven by a desire to prove himself and establish his own kingdom, he seeks to conquer Persia, unaware that his father is the land's greatest protector. This ambition, fueled by youthful ardor and a lack of knowledge about his lineage, sets the stage for the tragic events that follow. His upbringing shapes him into a figure of both immense potential and tragic vulnerability, a hero destined for greatness but ultimately undone by fate and circumstance.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Battles
extendedContent.content: Sohrab's quest to conquer Persia leads him into direct conflict with the Persian army, ultimately pitting him against Rostam. His most notable battle is against the champion of Persia, whom he does not know is his father. The encounter is marked by intense combat and mutual respect, as both warriors recognize the other's exceptional skill. Despite opportunities to reveal their identities, fate conspires to keep them ignorant of their kinship. Sohrab's prowess in battle is undeniable, and he nearly defeats Rostam on several occasions. However, Rostam, aided by his experience and cunning, manages to wound Sohrab mortally. As Sohrab lies dying, he reveals a token from his mother, Tahmineh, finally revealing his true identity. This recognition comes too late, sealing the tragic fate of both father and son and highlighting the devastating consequences of ignorance and war.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Enduring Significance
extendedContent.content: Sohrab's legacy is one of tragic heroism. His story serves as a poignant reminder of the destructive power of ignorance, the importance of communication, and the inevitability of fate. He embodies the potential for greatness cut short by circumstance, and his death leaves a lasting impact on Rostam, who is consumed by grief and remorse. Sohrab's tale continues to resonate in Persian culture, inspiring countless works of art, literature, and music. He remains a symbol of youthful courage, tragic destiny, and the enduring bonds of family. His story serves as a cautionary tale, reminding audiences of the importance of understanding and the devastating consequences of conflict and unrecognized kinship. The epic of Sohrab and Rostam is a cornerstone of Persian literature and a testament to the enduring power of myth.

shortDescription: Sohrab is a tragic hero in Persian mythology, son of Rostam, who unknowingly battles and is killed by his father. His story highlights fate, duty, and tragic ignorance.
